original_abstract:
Diabetic nephropathy (DN) remains a critical complication of diabetes and a leading cause of end-stage renal disease. While Rhein, a bioactive anthraquinone derivative, has demonstrated therapeutic promise in kidney disease, the molecular mechanisms driving its protective effects are not yet fully understood. This study investigated the impact of Rhein on podocyte injury and renal fibrosis using high glucose (HG)-induced podocyte models and streptozocin (STZ)-induced DN mice.

Our results demonstrate that Rhein treatment significantly improves podocyte viability and mitigates HG-induced damage. Specifically, Rhein suppressed ferroptosis, as evidenced by the reduced accumulation of lipid reactive oxygen species (ROS), malondialdehyde (MDA), and intracellular ferrous ions (Fe²⁺). This was accompanied by the downregulation of ferroptotic markers, including Transferrin Receptor 1 (TFR1) and acyl-CoA synthetase long-chain family member 4 (ACSL4), alongside the upregulation of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) and SLC7A11. Furthermore, Rhein effectively inhibited the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) process, reducing the expression of mesenchymal markers such as α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA), Vimentin, Snail, and Desmin, while preserving the epithelial marker Nephrin.

Mechanistically, we identified that Rhein suppresses the activation of the Ras-related C3 botulinum toxin substrate 1 (Rac1) pathway and its downstream effectors, NADPH Oxidase 1 (NOX1) and β-catenin. Functional assays revealed that silencing Rac1 mimicked the protective effects of Rhein, whereas Rac1 overexpression (oe-Rac1) or lentiviral delivery (LV-Rac1) neutralized Rhein’s benefits by promoting ferroptosis and EMT-driven fibrosis in vivo. In DN mice, Rhein administration alleviated renal morphological lesions, tubulointerstitial fibrosis, and biochemical markers of kidney dysfunction.

In conclusion, Rhein attenuates the progression of diabetic nephropathy by inhibiting ferroptosis and EMT through the regulation of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling axis. These findings provide a robust mechanical basis for the clinical development of Rhein as a targeted therapy for DN.
humanized_abstract:
Diabetic nephropathy (DN) is one of the most serious complications of diabetes and is a major cause of end-stage renal disease. Rhein, a bioactive anthraquinone derivative, has been reported to exert beneficial effects on kidney disease. However, the underlying molecular mechanisms remain unclear. The present study aimed to investigate the effects of Rhein on podocyte injury and renal fibrosis in vitro using a high glucose (HG) stimulated podocyte model and in vivo using streptozocin (STZ) induced diabetic nephropathy (DN) mice.

Our data showed that Rhein treated podocytes showed higher viability and resistance to HG-induced injury. Rhein suppressed ferroptosis by regulating lipid ROS, MDA, Fe2+ levels in cells. TFR1 and ACSL4 expression was decreased, while GPX4 and SLC7A11 expression was increased. Rhein suppressed the EMT process in podocytes by downregulating α-SMA, Vimentin, Snail and Desmin expressions, and upregulating Nephrin expression.

Rhein inhibited the activation of Rac1 and subsequently suppressed the expression of NOX1 and β-catenin. The in vivo caspase activity inhibition assay further confirmed that Rhein inhibited ferroptosis through inactivating Rac1. Rac1 siRNA mimicked the protective effects of Rhein, while oe-Rac1 and LV-Rac1 abolished the Rhein-mediated inhibition of ferroptosis and EMT-driven fibrosis in vivo. Rhein treated DN mice showed improved renal pathological structure, reduced fibrosis in the tubulointerstitium and lower levels of serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen.

Rhein suppressed the progression of diabetic nephropathy (DN) through non-ferroptotic inhibition of autophagy and the epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) process by inactivating the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway. This study revealed a robust molecular rationale for using Rhein as a specific therapeutical reagent for DN.

Diabetic nephropathy (DN) remains a critical complication of diabetes and a leading cause of end-stage renal disease. While Rhein, a bioactive anthraquinone derivative, has demonstrated therapeutic promise in kidney disease, the molecular mechanisms driving its protective effects are not yet fully understood.
